content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kristin Pelletier 
Acting City Attorney 
City of Anaheim 
200 South Anaheim Blvd. #356 
Anaheim, CA 92805 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-127 
Dear Ms. Pelletier: 
June 28, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Anaheim City Councilmembefs 
Lucille Kring and Jordan Brandman regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Rcfonn Act (the "Act").1 
This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission 
("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
Docs the Act prohibit Councilmembers Kring or Brandman from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to short-tenn rentals of residential properties ("STRs") given that 
each Councilmember owns a residence within 500 feet of a permitted STR and an STR applicant? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmembers from taking part in decisions relating to 
STRs because the financial effect of the decisions on either Councilmembers' residence is 
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-127 
Page No. 2 
You are the Acting City Attorney for the City of Anaheim and the authorized representative 
of City Councilmembers Lucille Kring and Jordan Brandman. The Anaheim Municipal Code 
authorizes STRs subject to the City's rules, regulations, and permitting process. A moratorium is 
currently in effect prohibiting the City from approving any application for a STR permit received 
after the moratorium's effective date while the City studies the future impacts and fate of STRs. 
The City Council has requested that Staff prepare two ordinances for its consideration at an 
upcoming public meeting. The first proposed ordinance would prohibit future STRs in residential 
zones, allow current STR permit holders to continue their STRs without a fixed expiration date, and 
allow the City to issue STR permits to applicants whose applications were pending when the 
moratorium went into effect. In addition, this proposed ordinance would tighten the rules applicable 
to STR operators by, among other things, limiting off-site parking, imposing occupancy limits, and 
establishing conduct regulations for STR occupants. 
The second proposed ordinance, which would be considered only if the first proposed 
ordinance is adopted, would establish an expiration date for all STR permits and would potentially 
allow a further amortization period for STR permit holders to recoup their investment cost, after 
which STRs would be prohibited. Both proposed ordinances would allow for revocation of STR 
permits under specified circumstances, and would have City-wide application. 
There are 50,631 residential parcels in the City, of which 370 have been issued STR permits 
or had license applications pending prior to the enactment of the moratorium. Approximately 20 
percent of City residential parcels have a STR located within 500 feet of them. If that distance is 
increased to 1 ,000 feet, approximately one-third of City residential parcels have a STR within that 
radius. 
Neither Councilmember Kring nor Councilmember Brandman holds or has submitted an 
application for a STR permit. Councilmember Kring's residence is within approximately 476 feet of 
a permitted STR, and shares property lines with four other residential parcels, one of which is a 
STR applicant. Councilmember Brandman's residence is approximately 20 feet away, across an 
alley, from a permitted STR, and within 367 feet of a STR applicant. 
STRs are highly concentrated in the western half of the City, around the Disneyland Resort. 
Of the 370 City residential parcels with STR permits or pending STR applications, all but 3 parcels 
with STR permits and 2 with STR applications are located west of the Orange Freeway, which runs 
north and south and roughly bifurcates the western and eastern halves of the City. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family that is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Different standards apply to determine whether a 


File No. A-16-127 
Page No. 3 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the 
interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. 
Under Sections 82033 and 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property owned 
directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the 
interest has a fair market value of $2,000 or more. The Councilmembers' only interests implicated 
by the facts presented and the decisions relating to STRs are their real property interests in their 
respective residences. 
Accordingly, we must determine whether the decisions at issue would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmembers' real property interests distinguishable 
from their effect on the public generally. 
Foreseeability & Materiality 
Regulation 18701(b) provides that a financial effect on an official's financial interest is 
reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical; a financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable. 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1) provides that an effect is material if the decision affects "real 
property value located within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property .... 
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official 
to participate under these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts 
to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's 
property." 
STRs are controversial because they can result in higher occupancies, increased noise, 
parking deficienciesÈ and waste disposal concerns, among other issues, in residential 
neighborhoods. The decisions at issue could result in a prohibition on new STRs, further regulation 
of existing STRs, or the establishment of an expiration date for all STR permits, after which STRs 
would be prohibited. The change of use of property within close proximity to a real property 
interest could affect that interest's value. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions at 
issue could have a material financial effect on the Councilmembers' respective real property 
interests in their residences, and the Councilmembers are disqualified from taking part in the 
decisions unless their effects on the Councilmembers' interests are indistinguishable from their 
effects on the public generally. 
Public Generally Exception 
The Act prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the decision 
on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally exception," Regulation l 8703(a) sets forth the 
general rule: 


File No. A-16-127 
Page No. 4 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on 
the significant segment." 
Significant Segment? 
For purposes of the exception, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 
percent of ( l) the businesses or non-profit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, 
or residential real property; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 
I 8703(b).) 
Under Re1::,111lation I 8703(b ), the "significant segment of the public" with respect to the 
decisions at issue is 25 percent of the residential real property within the City. There are 50,631 
residential parcels in the City, of which 370 have associated STR permits or had associated permit 
applications pending prior to the enactment of the moratorium. Approximately 20 percent of 
residential parcels in the City have a STR located within 500 feet of them, and if that distance is 
increased to 1,000 feet, approximately one-third of City residential parcels have a STR within that 
radius. The potential externalities associated with STRs, such as higher occupancies, increased 
noise, parking deficiencies, and waste disposal concerns, are noÉ limited to within 500 feet of an 
STR and likely reach 1,000 feet and beyond. Therefore, we find that at least 25 percent of the 
residential real property within the City will be affected by the decisions. 
Unique Effect? 
Under Regulation 18703(c), a "unique effect" on a public official's financial interest 
includes a disproportionate effect on: 
"(I) The development potential or use of the official's real property or 
on the income producing potential of the official's real property or business 
entity. 
"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the 
proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
"(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties 
resulting from the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in 
similar entities or properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a 
single interest. 
"(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting 
from the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property 
size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or 
percentage. 


File No. A-16-127 
Page No. 5 
"(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real 
property if the person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate 
family." (Regulation l 8703(c).) 
None of the specific criteria above apply. Moreover, neither Councilmember Kring nor 
Councilmember Brandman holds or has submitted an application for a STR permit. Councilmember 
K.ring's residence is within approximately 476 feet of a permitted STR, and shares property lines 
with four other residential parcels, one of which is a STR applicant. Councilmember Brandman's 
residence is approximately 20 feet away, across an alley, from a permitted STR, and within 367 feet 
of a STR applicant. STRs are highly concentrated in the western half of the City, around the 
Disneyland Resort. Of the 370 residential parcels with STR permits or pending STR applications 
within the City, approximately 98.6 percent of those parcels are located west of the Orange 
Freeway, which runs north and south and roughly bifurcates the western and eastern halves of the 
City. Because both Councilmembers' residences are located within the western half of the City, it is 
not unique for either of their residences to have multiple parcels with STR permits or STR 
applications nearby. Therefore, we find that the decisions at issue would not have a unique effect on 
either Councilmember's residence. 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit Councilmembers Kring and 
Brandman from taking part in decisions relating to STRs because the financial effect of the 
decisions on either Councilmember's real property interest in his or her residence is 
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division
